You can tell a senior developer from a junior in ninety seconds.
Not from their resume.
Not from how fast they type.
From three habits. Quiet ones. The kind that never show up in a demo but decide everything six months later.
I learned this watching two developers on my team receive the same feature ticket. Same requirements, same deadline. The junior shipped in two days and we spent the next month paying for it. The senior shipped in four days and we never touched that code again.
The gap between them was not talent. It was not even knowledge. It was habits, and habits can be copied. That is the good news buried in this article: you do not need five more years of experience to work like a senior. You need three specific behaviors, starting today.
Here they are, with the receipts.
Habit 1: Seniors Type the Edges Before They Write the Feature
Watch a junior start a feature: they open the component file and start writing HTML.
Watch a senior start the same feature: they open a blank file and write types first. Not code. Types.
